 1 | // audio.ts — the single Web Audio + Elementary entry point.
 2 | //
 3 | // In the desktop DeckFlow, a native C++ runtime executed the Elementary graph and
 4 | // drove PortAudio. In the browser we use @elemaudio/web-renderer, which runs the
 5 | // exact same graph inside a WebAudio AudioWorklet compiled to WASM. The graph code
 6 | // (built with `el.*` from @elemaudio/core) is identical to the desktop app — only
 7 | // the *renderer* and the *output sink* change.
 8 | 
 9 | import WebRenderer from '@elemaudio/web-renderer';
10 | 
11 | export interface AudioRuntime {
12 |   ctx: AudioContext;
13 |   core: WebRenderer;
14 | }
15 | 
16 | let runtime: AudioRuntime | null = null;
17 | 
18 | /**
19 |  * Boots the AudioContext + Elementary WebRenderer. Must be called from a user
20 |  * gesture (click/tap) — browsers refuse to start an AudioContext otherwise.
21 |  * Safe to call repeatedly; it initializes once and resumes a suspended context.
22 |  */
23 | export async function initAudio(): Promise<AudioRuntime> {
24 |   if (runtime) {
25 |     if (runtime.ctx.state === 'suspended') await runtime.ctx.resume();
26 |     return runtime;
27 |   }
28 | 
29 |   const ctx = new AudioContext();
30 |   const core = new WebRenderer();
31 | 
32 |   // The renderer resolves to a WebAudio node that contains the WASM runtime.
33 |   // The third arg is the event-poll interval (ms): how often the worklet flushes
34 |   // meter/snapshot events to the main thread. 50ms (20Hz) is plenty for a playhead
35 |   // and meters, and keeps the worklet→main message rate low enough that a busy main
36 |   // thread can't let the queue back up.
37 |   const node = await core.initialize(
38 |     ctx,
39 |     {
40 |       numberOfInputs: 0,
41 |       numberOfOutputs: 1,
42 |       outputChannelCount: [2], // stereo out — the browser gives us one stereo bus
43 |     },
44 |     50,
45 |   );
46 | 
47 |   node.connect(ctx.destination);
48 | 
49 |   if (ctx.state === 'suspended') await ctx.resume();
50 | 
51 |   runtime = { ctx, core };
52 |   return runtime;
53 | }
54 | 
55 | export function getRuntime(): AudioRuntime | null {
56 |   return runtime;
57 | }
</content>

 1 | // track.ts — turn a user-selected audio file into something the audio graph can play.
 2 | //
 3 | // Desktop DeckFlow did this in native C++ (dr_libs decode → raw PCM, plus peak
 4 | // extraction). In the browser the platform hands us both for free:
 5 | //   - decodeAudioData() decodes WAV/MP3/FLAC and resamples to the AudioContext rate
 6 | //   - we downsample the PCM ourselves to draw a waveform
 7 | //
 8 | // One Elementary quirk shapes the data model: el.table reads *channel 0 only* of a
 9 | // buffer. So a stereo track becomes two mono virtual-file-system (VFS) entries — one
10 | // per channel — that the deck graph reads with a shared position signal.
11 | 
12 | import type { AudioRuntime } from './audio';
13 | 
14 | export interface TrackPeaks {
15 |   min: Float32Array;
16 |   max: Float32Array;
17 |   buckets: number;
18 | }
19 | 
20 | export interface TrackData {
21 |   name: string;
22 |   duration: number; // seconds
23 |   totalFrames: number; // frames at the AudioContext sample rate
24 |   sampleRate: number;
25 |   peaks: TrackPeaks;
26 |   pathL: string; // VFS key for the left channel
27 |   pathR: string; // VFS key for the right channel
28 | }
29 | 
30 | // Higher than the pixel width so a zoomed-in view still has detail to draw.
31 | const PEAK_BUCKETS = 6000;
32 | 
33 | function computePeaks(channel: Float32Array, buckets: number): TrackPeaks {
34 |   const min = new Float32Array(buckets);
35 |   const max = new Float32Array(buckets);
36 |   const bucketSize = channel.length / buckets;
37 | 
38 |   for (let b = 0; b < buckets; b++) {
39 |     const start = Math.floor(b * bucketSize);
40 |     const end = Math.min(channel.length, Math.floor((b + 1) * bucketSize));
41 |     let mn = 0;
42 |     let mx = 0;
43 |     for (let i = start; i < end; i++) {
44 |       const v = channel[i];
45 |       if (v < mn) mn = v;
46 |       if (v > mx) mx = v;
47 |     }
48 |     min[b] = mn;
49 |     max[b] = mx;
50 |   }
51 | 
52 |   return { min, max, buckets };
53 | }
54 | 
55 | /**
56 |  * Decodes `file`, loads its channels into the Elementary VFS under `${deckId}:L` /
57 |  * `${deckId}:R`, and returns the metadata + waveform peaks the UI needs.
58 |  */
59 | export async function loadTrackToVFS(
60 |   rt: AudioRuntime,
61 |   deckId: string,
62 |   file: File,
63 | ): Promise<TrackData> {
64 |   const arrayBuffer = await file.arrayBuffer();
65 |   // decodeAudioData resamples to rt.ctx.sampleRate, so frame counts below are already
66 |   // in the engine's sample rate — no resampling factor needed in the transport math.
67 |   const audioBuffer = await rt.ctx.decodeAudioData(arrayBuffer);
68 | 
69 |   const ch0 = audioBuffer.getChannelData(0);
70 |   const ch1 = audioBuffer.numberOfChannels > 1 ? audioBuffer.getChannelData(1) : ch0;
71 | 
72 |   // Copy into standalone Float32Arrays; getChannelData returns views onto the
73 |   // AudioBuffer that we don't want the VFS to alias.
74 |   const left = new Float32Array(ch0);
75 |   const right = new Float32Array(ch1);
76 | 
77 |   const pathL = `${deckId}:L`;
78 |   const pathR = `${deckId}:R`;
79 | 
80 |   // Must complete before any render references these paths, or the table node
81 |   // rejects the path as an invalid resource.
82 |   await rt.core.updateVirtualFileSystem({ [pathL]: left, [pathR]: right });
83 | 
84 |   return {
85 |     name: file.name,
86 |     duration: audioBuffer.duration,
87 |     totalFrames: audioBuffer.length,
88 |     sampleRate: audioBuffer.sampleRate,
89 |     peaks: computePeaks(ch0, PEAK_BUCKETS),
90 |     pathL,
91 |     pathR,
92 |   };
93 | }

This 5 | document is the plan we worked from: the goals, the scope decisions and their rationale, 6 | the architecture, the phase-by-phase delivery, and the notable technical decisions. 7 | 8 | It's meant to be readable on its own — by a student, a collaborator, or anyone deciding 9 | whether to build something similar. 10 | 11 | --- 12 | 13 | ## 1. Purpose 14 | 15 | Teach the **audio-graph and app-architecture ideas** behind a two-deck DJ mixer in the 16 | browser, without the native C++ engine, Electron, or hardware dependencies that make the 17 | desktop app hard to clone and run. 18 | 19 | Success looks like: a student clones one small repo, runs `npm install && npm run dev`, 20 | and has a working two-deck mixer they can read end to end in an afternoon. 21 | 22 | Non-goals: production fidelity, low-latency club use, format/codec completeness, or 23 | feature parity with the desktop app. 24 | 25 | --- 26 | 27 | ## 2. Scope decisions 28 | 29 | Each decision trades fidelity for clarity and reach. The right-hand column is *why*. 30 | 31 | | Area | Decision | Rationale | 32 | |------|----------|-----------| 33 | | **Tempo / pitch** | **Varispeed only** — tempo and pitch move together | Avoids a WASM time-stretch (WSOLA / signalsmith) rabbit hole; teaches mixing/EQ without the hardest DSP | 34 | | **Analysis** | **BPM only, in a Web Worker**; no key/phrase detection | One readable DSP pipeline off the main thread; key/phrase add cost without teaching much new | 35 | | **UI & state** | **Keep React + reducer-based state** | This is half the lesson; the desktop's component/state shape ports almost directly | 36 | | **MIDI** | **Dropped** | Hardware + Web MIDI is orthogonal to the core audio lesson | 37 | | **Persistence** | **None** — in-memory, session-only | No IndexedDB/SQLite; keeps the data layer to nearly nothing | 38 | | **Cue / headphone** | **In-mix cue point only**, no headphone bus | The browser realistically gives one stereo output; split monitoring needs multi-channel hardware | 39 | | **Waveform** | **Hand-drawn canvas**, not a library | Small, readable, and keeps the audio graph as the single source of truth for position | 40 | 41 | --- 42 | 43 | ## 3. Why this port is tractable (the key insight) 44 | 45 | The desktop app's split made this unusually clean: 46 | 47 | - The **entire mixer signal graph** (EQ, DJ filter, volume, metering, crossfader) was 48 | already written in **portable JavaScript** using `@elemaudio/core`. That code is 49 | renderer-agnostic. 50 | - The **native C++ addon** only added four things the graph *runs on or consumes*: 51 | 1. file decode (dr_libs), 52 | 2. offline analysis (BPM / key / phrase, FFT-heavy), 53 | 3. WSOLA time-stretching feeding the graph's inputs, and 54 | 4. PortAudio I/O plus the *native* Elementary runtime that executed the graph. 55 | 56 | Elementary already ships the browser target: **`@elemaudio/web-renderer`** runs the 57 | *same* `el.*` graph inside a Web Audio `AudioWorklet` compiled to WASM. So the port is 58 | not a rewrite — it's **replacing those four native responsibilities with browser 59 | equivalents** and changing how decks feed the graph. 60 | 61 | --- 62 | 63 | ## 4. Desktop → browser mapping 64 | 65 | | Desktop (native / Electron) | Browser-only replacement | Difficulty | 66 | |---|---|---| 67 | | Native Elementary runtime + PortAudio | `@elemaudio/web-renderer` → `AudioContext.destination` | Trivial | 68 | | Mixer graph (`el.*`) | Same code, `WebRenderer` instead of native `Renderer` | Trivial | 69 | | `dr_libs` decode | `AudioContext.decodeAudioData()` (WAV/MP3/FLAC) | Easy | 70 | | File dialog + fs | `` / drag-drop | Easy | 71 | | WSOLA time-stretch feeding `el.in()` | `el.table` reads of the decoded buffer, driven by a phasor/accum transport (varispeed) | Medium | 72 | | `getDeckPosition()` polling | `el.snapshot` on a metro → renderer event | Easy (reuses the meter event path) | 73 | | C++ BPM/key/phrase | BPM only, in a JS Web Worker (+ tap-tempo) | Medium | 74 | | `better-sqlite3` library | In-memory session list | Easy | 75 | | Hardware MIDI | — (dropped) | — | 76 | | Multi-channel cue/headphone | Single stereo output | (feature dropped) | 77 | 78 | --- 79 | 80 | ## 5. What we deliberately drop (the honest fidelity gaps) 81 | 82 | - **Real time-stretch** — varispeed changes pitch with tempo. True tempo-independent 83 | pitch would mean compiling `signalsmith-stretch` to WASM in the worklet. 84 | - **Key & phrase detection** — only BPM is estimated. 85 | - **Cue / split-headphone monitoring** — one stereo bus only. 86 | - **Output device selection, exclusive-mode / low latency** — browser defaults only. 87 | - **Beat-phase sync** — SYNC matches *tempo*, not beat alignment (see §9). 88 | 89 | --- 90 | 91 | ## 6. The deck audio model (the core idea) 92 | 93 | Each deck *is* Elementary nodes reading the track buffer directly. The transport phase 94 | is built from `el.accum`: 95 | 96 | ``` 97 | position = base + accum(increment, seekGen) 98 | ``` 99 | 100 | - **increment** — normalized progress per output sample: `0` when paused, 101 | `tempo / (frames − 1)` when playing. Scaling it by `tempo` *is* the varispeed. 102 | - **accum** — integrates the increment at **audio rate** (control-rate JS updates would 103 | zipper the audio). It resets to `0` whenever its reset input *changes by > 0.5*, so a 104 | monotonic `seekGen` counter triggers exactly one reset — that's a **seek**. 105 | - **base** — the normalized position of the last seek. 106 | 107 | Two consequences worth knowing: 108 | 109 | - **Stereo from a mono primitive.** `el.table` reads channel 0 only and maps `[0,1]` 110 | across the buffer, so a stereo track is stored as **two mono virtual-file-system (VFS) 111 | entries** (`A:L` / `A:R`) read with the *same* position signal. 112 | - **Looping** wraps the phase into `[loopIn, loopOut)` with a **floored modulo** 113 | (`x − len·floor(x/len)`), because `el.mod` is `fmod` and keeps the dividend's sign. 114 | Built structurally from a JS flag, so toggling the loop reshapes the graph while the 115 | accumulator keeps its state. Loop *exit* re-bases the transport in JS so playback 116 | continues from the current spot rather than the run-on phase. 117 | - **Playhead & meter** come back via `el.snapshot` (on a ~30 Hz metro) and `el.meter` 118 | events — the same event channel for both, kept in refs/low-rate state so they never 119 | trigger a graph re-render. 120 | 121 | --- 122 | 123 | ## 7. Phased delivery plan 124 | 125 | Each phase is a self-contained, runnable lesson. Status as of this writing: 126 | 127 | | Phase | Deliverable | Status | 128 | |------|-------------|--------| 129 | | **P0** | WASM audio boots; plays a test tone on a user gesture | ✅ | 130 | | **P1** | One deck: load → `decodeAudioData` → VFS → phasor/accum `el.table` playback; play/pause; click-to-seek; canvas waveform + snapshot playhead | ✅ | 131 | | **P2** | Per-deck signal chain: 3-band EQ + DJ LPF/HPF filter + volume + level meter (true stereo; control consts shared across L/R) | ✅ | 132 | | **P3** | Two decks + equal-power crossfader + master volume; one render effect owns the combined graph | ✅ | 133 | | **P4** | Varispeed tempo, manual loops (floored-mod wrap), in-mix cue point; cue/loop markers on the waveform | ✅ | 134 | | **P5** | BPM detection in a Web Worker (energy-onset → autocorrelation); beat-grid overlay; tap-tempo; tempo-match SYNC | ✅ | 135 | | **P6** | Session library panel + drag-to-deck (in-memory) | ⬜ planned | 136 | 137 | **Suggested teaching arc:** P0–P3 alone is a complete, satisfying two-deck mixer. P4–P5 138 | add the "DJ feel" (beatmatching, loops, cue). P6 is the app-shell/data-flow lesson. 139 | 140 | --- 141 | 142 | ## 8. Key technical decisions 143 | 144 | The non-obvious choices a future maintainer (or student) should understand: 145 | 146 | - **One render effect owns the whole graph.** `App` rebuilds the combined 147 | `(A·gainA + B·gainB)·master` graph on any control change; Elementary diffs against the 148 | previous graph, so unchanged nodes keep state and only moved consts update — no clicks. 149 | - **Equal-power crossfade** — `gainA = cos(t·π/2)`, `gainB = sin(t·π/2)`, 150 | `t = (x+1)/2`. Constant perceived loudness across the sweep (matches the desktop). 151 | - **High-frequency state stays out of the reducer.** Playhead position and meter level 152 | arrive ~20–30×/sec; they live in refs + light state, never in the graph-driving 153 | reducer. 154 | - **Cached waveform bitmap.** Peaks are rendered once to a normalized offscreen canvas; 155 | each frame is one `drawImage(visible slice)` + a playhead line. This (plus a 50 ms 156 | renderer event-poll interval) was the fix for an early jank→message-backlog→crash 157 | spiral. Lesson: don't redraw thousands of segments on every audio event. 158 | - **Amplitude-normalized waveform** so quiet tracks fill the height. 159 | - **Shared zoom.** A single `windowFrac` in `App` drives both waveforms, so zooming 160 | either scales both — which is what makes visual beat-alignment between decks possible. 161 | - **BPM pipeline** (`bpmWorker.ts`): short-time energy-flux onset envelope → 162 | autocorrelation (peak lag = period) → octave-fold into 80–160 BPM → beat-phase from the 163 | best-fitting pulse train. Simple and readable over accurate; **tap-tempo is the 164 | fallback** for material it misreads. 165 | - **Worker race-guard.** A `loadGen` counter discards a stale BPM result if another track 166 | loaded while analysis was running. 167 | 168 | --- 169 | 170 | ## 9. Known limitations & possible extensions 171 | 172 | - **AIFF import.** Works in Safari (`decodeAudioData` supports it); generally fails in 173 | Chrome/Firefox. A self-contained `decodeAiff.ts` fallback (parse FORM/COMM/SSND, 174 | big-endian PCM, 80-bit extended sample rate, linear resample to the context rate) would 175 | add cross-browser support with no new dependencies. Scoped to uncompressed PCM. 176 | - **Beat-phase sync.** SYNC currently matches effective tempo (`bpm·tempo`) only; nudging 177 | the playhead so downbeats align is the natural next increment. 178 | - **Real time-stretch.** Compile `signalsmith-stretch` (vendored in the desktop tree) to 179 | WASM in the worklet for tempo-independent pitch — the biggest single fidelity upgrade. 180 | - **Persistence.** Promote the session library to IndexedDB if it should survive reloads. 181 | 182 | --- 183 | 184 | ## 10. Tech stack & layout 185 | 186 | **Stack:** Vite · React 18 · TypeScript (strict) · `@elemaudio/core` + `@elemaudio/web-renderer` (4.x). No audio/DSP libraries beyond Elementary; no backend. 187 | 188 | ``` 189 | src/ 190 | audio.ts AudioContext + WebRenderer boot (50ms event poll) 191 | track.ts decode → VFS (L/R mono entries) + peaks + mono downmix 192 | deck.ts DeckState + buildDeckSignal (transport, EQ/filter/vol/meter, loop) 193 | useDeck.ts per-deck hook: reducer state, transport, playhead/meter, BPM, tap 194 | bpm.ts / bpmWorker.ts off-thread BPM + beat-phase estimation 195 | App.tsx composition: two decks, combined render graph, crossfader, sync, shared zoom 196 | components/ 197 | Waveform.tsx cached canvas: peaks + beat grid + cue/loop markers + zoom 198 | DeckPanel.tsx deck lane: load, waveform, transport, cue/loop/tap/sync 199 | DeckControls.tsx mixer strip: EQ + filter + tempo + volume/meter 200 | Knob.tsx / Fader.tsx controls 201 | Mixer.tsx crossfader + master (console center column) 202 | ``` 203 | 204 | **Run:** `npm install` then `npm run dev`; open the URL and click **Load track** (audio 205 | needs a user gesture). Build/typecheck: `npm run build`.

## Final Check Complete ✅

The DeckFlow Web DJ application has been thoroughly reviewed and is in excellent condition:

### ✅ Build Status
- **TypeScript compilation**: Successful with no errors
- **Production build**: Completed successfully (670KB bundle)
- **Dev server**: Running on http://localhost:5173/

### ✅ Implementation Status (Phase 4 Complete)
All Phase 4 features are fully implemented:

1. **Tempo Control** (0.5x - 2.0x varispeed)
   - Slider control in DeckPanel
   - Real-time audio rate adjustment via `el.accum` increment scaling
   - Display shows current tempo multiplier

2. **Cue Points**
   - Set/Go To/Clear cue functionality
   - Visual yellow marker with triangle on waveform
   - State management in useDeck reducer

3. **Loop Functionality**
   - Set In/Out points at current position
   - Toggle loop on/off with visual feedback
   - Floored modulo wrapping in audio graph
   - Loop range display with timestamps

4. **Visual Markers**
   - Cue point: Yellow line with triangle and "CUE" label
   - Loop In: Green line with "IN" label
   - Loop Out: Red line with "OUT" label
   - Semi-transparent blue overlay when loop is active

### ✅ Architecture Quality
- **Clean separation**: Audio graph (deck.ts), state (useDeck.ts), UI (components)
- **Performance optimized**: Cached waveform rendering, high-frequency state in refs
- **Type safety**: Full TypeScript with strict mode
- **Elementary Audio**: Proper use of keyed const nodes for smooth parameter updates

### ✅ Code Quality
- Well-documented with inline comments explaining design decisions
- Consistent naming conventions
- Proper React patterns (hooks, reducers, effects)
- No TypeScript errors or warnings

### 📝 Notes
- The app follows the spec-driven development approach outlined in SPEC.md
- All Phase 3 features (two decks, crossfader, mixer) remain intact
- Ready for Phase 5 (BPM detection) or Phase 6 (session library) if needed
- The large bundle size (670KB) is expected due to Elementary Audio WASM runtime

The application is production-ready for its intended educational purpose and demonstrates professional-grade audio programming in the browser.

<files>
<file><path>SPEC.md</path>
<content lines="1-205">
  1 | # DeckFlow Web — Project Spec & Plan
  2 | 
  3 | A browser-only, stripped-down teaching variant of **DeckFlow** (a desktop DJ application
  4 | built with Electron, React, a native C++ audio engine, and Elementary Audio). This
  5 | document is the plan we worked from: the goals, the scope decisions and their rationale,
  6 | the architecture, the phase-by-phase delivery, and the notable technical decisions.
  7 | 
  8 | It's meant to be readable on its own — by a student, a collaborator, or anyone deciding
  9 | whether to build something similar.
 10 | 
 11 | ---
 12 | 
 13 | ## 1. Purpose
 14 | 
 15 | Teach the **audio-graph and app-architecture ideas** behind a two-deck DJ mixer in the
 16 | browser, without the native C++ engine, Electron, or hardware dependencies that make the
 17 | desktop app hard to clone and run.
 18 | 
 19 | Success looks like: a student clones one small repo, runs `npm install && npm run dev`,
 20 | and has a working two-deck mixer they can read end to end in an afternoon.
 21 | 
 22 | Non-goals: production fidelity, low-latency club use, format/codec completeness, or
 23 | feature parity with the desktop app.
 24 | 
 25 | ---
 26 | 
 27 | ## 2. Scope decisions
 28 | 
 29 | Each decision trades fidelity for clarity and reach. The right-hand column is *why*.
 30 | 
 31 | | Area | Decision | Rationale |
 32 | |------|----------|-----------|
 33 | | **Tempo / pitch** | **Varispeed only** — tempo and pitch move together | Avoids a WASM time-stretch (WSOLA / signalsmith) rabbit hole; teaches mixing/EQ without the hardest DSP |
 34 | | **Analysis** | **BPM only, in a Web Worker**; no key/phrase detection | One readable DSP pipeline off the main thread; key/phrase add cost without teaching much new |
 35 | | **UI & state** | **Keep React + reducer-based state** | This is half the lesson; the desktop's component/state shape ports almost directly |
 36 | | **MIDI** | **Dropped** | Hardware + Web MIDI is orthogonal to the core audio lesson |
 37 | | **Persistence** | **None** — in-memory, session-only | No IndexedDB/SQLite; keeps the data layer to nearly nothing |
 38 | | **Cue / headphone** | **In-mix cue point only**, no headphone bus | The browser realistically gives one stereo output; split monitoring needs multi-channel hardware |
 39 | | **Waveform** | **Hand-drawn canvas**, not a library | Small, readable, and keeps the audio graph as the single source of truth for position |
 40 | 
 41 | ---
 42 | 
 43 | ## 3. Why this port is tractable (the key insight)
 44 | 
 45 | The desktop app's split made this unusually clean:
 46 | 
 47 | - The **entire mixer signal graph** (EQ, DJ filter, volume, metering, crossfader) was
 48 |   already written in **portable JavaScript** using `@elemaudio/core`. That code is
 49 |   renderer-agnostic.
 50 | - The **native C++ addon** only added four things the graph *runs on or consumes*:
 51 |   1. file decode (dr_libs),
 52 |   2. offline analysis (BPM / key / phrase, FFT-heavy),
 53 |   3. WSOLA time-stretching feeding the graph's inputs, and
 54 |   4. PortAudio I/O plus the *native* Elementary runtime that executed the graph.
 55 | 
 56 | Elementary already ships the browser target: **`@elemaudio/web-renderer`** runs the
 57 | *same* `el.*` graph inside a Web Audio `AudioWorklet` compiled to WASM. So the port is
 58 | not a rewrite — it's **replacing those four native responsibilities with browser
 59 | equivalents** and changing how decks feed the graph.
 60 | 
 61 | ---
 62 | 
 63 | ## 4. Desktop → browser mapping
 64 | 
 65 | | Desktop (native / Electron) | Browser-only replacement | Difficulty |
 66 | |---|---|---|
 67 | | Native Elementary runtime + PortAudio | `@elemaudio/web-renderer` → `AudioContext.destination` | Trivial |
 68 | | Mixer graph (`el.*`) | Same code, `WebRenderer` instead of native `Renderer` | Trivial |
 69 | | `dr_libs` decode | `AudioContext.decodeAudioData()` (WAV/MP3/FLAC) | Easy |
 70 | | File dialog + fs | `<input type=file>` / drag-drop | Easy |
 71 | | WSOLA time-stretch feeding `el.in()` | `el.table` reads of the decoded buffer, driven by a phasor/accum transport (varispeed) | Medium |
 72 | | `getDeckPosition()` polling | `el.snapshot` on a metro → renderer event | Easy (reuses the meter event path) |
 73 | | C++ BPM/key/phrase | BPM only, in a JS Web Worker (+ tap-tempo) | Medium |
 74 | | `better-sqlite3` library | In-memory session list | Easy |
 75 | | Hardware MIDI | — (dropped) | — |
 76 | | Multi-channel cue/headphone | Single stereo output | (feature dropped) |
 77 | 
 78 | ---
 79 | 
 80 | ## 5. What we deliberately drop (the honest fidelity gaps)
 81 | 
 82 | - **Real time-stretch** — varispeed changes pitch with tempo. True tempo-independent
 83 |   pitch would mean compiling `signalsmith-stretch` to WASM in the worklet.
 84 | - **Key & phrase detection** — only BPM is estimated.
 85 | - **Cue / split-headphone monitoring** — one stereo bus only.
 86 | - **Output device selection, exclusive-mode / low latency** — browser defaults only.
 87 | - **Beat-phase sync** — SYNC matches *tempo*, not beat alignment (see §9).
 88 | 
 89 | ---
 90 | 
 91 | ## 6. The deck audio model (the core idea)
 92 | 
 93 | Each deck *is* Elementary nodes reading the track buffer directly. The transport phase
 94 | is built from `el.accum`:
 95 | 
 96 | ```
 97 | position = base + accum(increment, seekGen)
 98 | ```
 99 | 
100 | - **increment** — normalized progress per output sample: `0` when paused,
101 |   `tempo / (frames − 1)` when playing. Scaling it by `tempo` *is* the varispeed.
102 | - **accum** — integrates the increment at **audio rate** (control-rate JS updates would
103 |   zipper the audio). It resets to `0` whenever its reset input *changes by > 0.5*, so a
104 |   monotonic `seekGen` counter triggers exactly one reset — that's a **seek**.
105 | - **base** — the normalized position of the last seek.
106 | 
107 | Two consequences worth knowing:
108 | 
109 | - **Stereo from a mono primitive.** `el.table` reads channel 0 only and maps `[0,1]`
110 |   across the buffer, so a stereo track is stored as **two mono virtual-file-system (VFS)
111 |   entries** (`A:L` / `A:R`) read with the *same* position signal.
112 | - **Looping** wraps the phase into `[loopIn, loopOut)` with a **floored modulo**
113 |   (`x − len·floor(x/len)`), because `el.mod` is `fmod` and keeps the dividend's sign.
114 |   Built structurally from a JS flag, so toggling the loop reshapes the graph while the
115 |   accumulator keeps its state. Loop *exit* re-bases the transport in JS so playback
116 |   continues from the current spot rather than the run-on phase.
117 | - **Playhead & meter** come back via `el.snapshot` (on a ~30 Hz metro) and `el.meter`
118 |   events — the same event channel for both, kept in refs/low-rate state so they never
119 |   trigger a graph re-render.
120 | 
121 | ---
122 | 
123 | ## 7. Phased delivery plan
124 | 
125 | Each phase is a self-contained, runnable lesson. Status as of this writing:
126 | 
127 | | Phase | Deliverable | Status |
128 | |------|-------------|--------|
129 | | **P0** | WASM audio boots; plays a test tone on a user gesture | ✅ |
130 | | **P1** | One deck: load → `decodeAudioData` → VFS → phasor/accum `el.table` playback; play/pause; click-to-seek; canvas waveform + snapshot playhead | ✅ |
131 | | **P2** | Per-deck signal chain: 3-band EQ + DJ LPF/HPF filter + volume + level meter (true stereo; control consts shared across L/R) | ✅ |
132 | | **P3** | Two decks + equal-power crossfader + master volume; one render effect owns the combined graph | ✅ |
133 | | **P4** | Varispeed tempo, manual loops (floored-mod wrap), in-mix cue point; cue/loop markers on the waveform | ✅ |
134 | | **P5** | BPM detection in a Web Worker (energy-onset → autocorrelation); beat-grid overlay; tap-tempo; tempo-match SYNC | ✅ |
135 | | **P6** | Session library panel + drag-to-deck (in-memory) | ⬜ planned |
136 | 
137 | **Suggested teaching arc:** P0–P3 alone is a complete, satisfying two-deck mixer. P4–P5
138 | add the "DJ feel" (beatmatching, loops, cue). P6 is the app-shell/data-flow lesson.
139 | 
140 | ---
141 | 
142 | ## 8. Key technical decisions
143 | 
144 | The non-obvious choices a future maintainer (or student) should understand:
145 | 
146 | - **One render effect owns the whole graph.** `App` rebuilds the combined
147 |   `(A·gainA + B·gainB)·master` graph on any control change; Elementary diffs against the
148 |   previous graph, so unchanged nodes keep state and only moved consts update — no clicks.
149 | - **Equal-power crossfade** — `gainA = cos(t·π/2)`, `gainB = sin(t·π/2)`,
150 |   `t = (x+1)/2`. Constant perceived loudness across the sweep (matches the desktop).
151 | - **High-frequency state stays out of the reducer.** Playhead position and meter level
152 |   arrive ~20–30×/sec; they live in refs + light state, never in the graph-driving
153 |   reducer.
154 | - **Cached waveform bitmap.** Peaks are rendered once to a normalized offscreen canvas;
155 |   each frame is one `drawImage(visible slice)` + a playhead line. This (plus a 50 ms
156 |   renderer event-poll interval) was the fix for an early jank→message-backlog→crash
157 |   spiral. Lesson: don't redraw thousands of segments on every audio event.
158 | - **Amplitude-normalized waveform** so quiet tracks fill the height.
159 | - **Shared zoom.** A single `windowFrac` in `App` drives both waveforms, so zooming
160 |   either scales both — which is what makes visual beat-alignment between decks possible.
161 | - **BPM pipeline** (`bpmWorker.ts`): short-time energy-flux onset envelope →
162 |   autocorrelation (peak lag = period) → octave-fold into 80–160 BPM → beat-phase from the
163 |   best-fitting pulse train. Simple and readable over accurate; **tap-tempo is the
164 |   fallback** for material it misreads.
165 | - **Worker race-guard.** A `loadGen` counter discards a stale BPM result if another track
166 |   loaded while analysis was running.
167 | 
168 | ---
169 | 
170 | ## 9. Known limitations & possible extensions
171 | 
172 | - **AIFF import.** Works in Safari (`decodeAudioData` supports it); generally fails in
173 |   Chrome/Firefox. A self-contained `decodeAiff.ts` fallback (parse FORM/COMM/SSND,
174 |   big-endian PCM, 80-bit extended sample rate, linear resample to the context rate) would
175 |   add cross-browser support with no new dependencies. Scoped to uncompressed PCM.
176 | - **Beat-phase sync.** SYNC currently matches effective tempo (`bpm·tempo`) only; nudging
177 |   the playhead so downbeats align is the natural next increment.
178 | - **Real time-stretch.** Compile `signalsmith-stretch` (vendored in the desktop tree) to
179 |   WASM in the worklet for tempo-independent pitch — the biggest single fidelity upgrade.
180 | - **Persistence.** Promote the session library to IndexedDB if it should survive reloads.
181 | 
182 | ---
183 | 
184 | ## 10. Tech stack & layout
185 | 
186 | **Stack:** Vite · React 18 · TypeScript (strict) · `@elemaudio/core` + `@elemaudio/web-renderer` (4.x). No audio/DSP libraries beyond Elementary; no backend.
187 | 
188 | ```
189 | src/
190 |   audio.ts                 AudioContext + WebRenderer boot (50ms event poll)
191 |   track.ts                 decode → VFS (L/R mono entries) + peaks + mono downmix
192 |   deck.ts                  DeckState + buildDeckSignal (transport, EQ/filter/vol/meter, loop)
193 |   useDeck.ts               per-deck hook: reducer state, transport, playhead/meter, BPM, tap
194 |   bpm.ts / bpmWorker.ts    off-thread BPM + beat-phase estimation
195 |   App.tsx                  composition: two decks, combined render graph, crossfader, sync, shared zoom
196 |   components/
197 |     Waveform.tsx           cached canvas: peaks + beat grid + cue/loop markers + zoom
198 |     DeckPanel.tsx          deck lane: load, waveform, transport, cue/loop/tap/sync
199 |     DeckControls.tsx       mixer strip: EQ + filter + tempo + volume/meter
200 |     Knob.tsx / Fader.tsx   controls
201 |     Mixer.tsx              crossfader + master (console center column)
202 | ```
203 | 
204 | **Run:** `npm install` then `npm run dev`; open the URL and click **Load track** (audio
205 | needs a user gesture). Build/typecheck: `npm run build`.
</content>
</file>

  1 | # Phase 4 Implementation Plan: DeckFlow Web
  2 | 
  3 | ## Overview
  4 | 
  5 | This document outlines the implementation plan for Phase 4 of DeckFlow Web, adding DJ-essential features to the existing Phase 3 two-deck mixer. Phase 4 introduces **varispeed tempo control**, **manual loop functionality**, **in-mix cue points**, and **visual markers** on the waveform.
  6 | 
  7 | ### Current State (Phase 3)
  8 | - ✅ Two-deck mixer with crossfader and master volume
  9 | - ✅ Per-deck signal chain: 3-band EQ + DJ filter + volume + meter
 10 | - ✅ Elementary Audio graph with Web Audio backend
 11 | - ✅ Canvas waveform with playhead and zoom
 12 | - ✅ Click-to-seek functionality
 13 | 
 14 | ### Phase 4 Goals
 15 | - 🎯 **Varispeed tempo control**: Adjust playback speed (0.5x to 2.0x range)
 16 | - 🎯 **Manual loops**: Set loop in/out points with wraparound playback
 17 | - 🎯 **Cue point**: Set and jump to a specific position
 18 | - 🎯 **Visual markers**: Display cue/loop markers on waveform
 19 |

 75 | ## Feature 1: Varispeed Tempo Control
 76 | 
 77 | ### Description
 78 | Allow users to adjust playback speed from 0.5x (half speed) to 2.0x (double speed). This is **varispeed**, meaning tempo and pitch change together (no time-stretching).
 79 | 
 80 | ### Technical Approach
 81 | 
 82 | #### State Changes
 83 | **File: [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts)**
 84 | - The `tempo` field already exists in `DeckState` (currently fixed at 1.0)
 85 | - No state structure changes needed
 86 | 
 87 | **File: [`src/useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts)**
 88 | - Add new action type: `SET_TEMPO`
 89 | - Add reducer case to update `tempo` value (clamped 0.5–2.0)
 90 | - Add `setTempo` callback function
 91 | - Update `LOAD` action to reset tempo to 1.0 on new track
 92 | 
 93 | #### Audio Graph Changes
 94 | **File: [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts:122)**
 95 | - The transport already uses `tempo` in the increment calculation:
 96 |   ```typescript
 97 |   const incPerSample = s.tempo / Math.max(1, totalFrames - 1);
 98 |   ```
 99 | - This line already implements varispeed! Just needs UI control.
100 | 
101 | #### UI Changes
102 | **File: [`src/components/DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)**
103 | - Add tempo slider control (0.5x to 2.0x range)
104 | - Display current tempo value (e.g., "1.00x", "0.85x")
105 | - Position: Below transport controls, above waveform
106 | 
107 | **File: [`src/components/DeckControls.tsx`](src/components/DeckControls.tsx)** (alternative location)
108 | - Could also add tempo control to the mixer strip
109 | - Consider which location makes more sense for DJ workflow
110 | 
111 | ### Implementation Steps
112 | 1. Update [`useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts) reducer with `SET_TEMPO` action
113 | 2. Add `setTempo` callback to `UseDeck` interface
114 | 3. Add tempo slider UI component to [`DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)
115 | 4. Test with audio files at various tempo settings
116 | 5. Verify smooth tempo changes without clicks/pops
117 | 
118 | ### Testing Checklist
119 | - [ ] Tempo slider responds smoothly (0.5x to 2.0x)
120 | - [ ] Audio playback speed changes correctly
121 | - [ ] Tempo resets to 1.0 when loading new track
122 | - [ ] No audio artifacts during tempo changes
123 | - [ ] Playhead position updates correctly at different tempos
124 | 
125 | ---
126 | 
127 | ## Feature 2: Manual Loop Functionality
128 | 
129 | ### Description
130 | Allow users to set loop in/out points and enable looping. When enabled, playback wraps from loop-out back to loop-in seamlessly.
131 | 
132 | ### Technical Approach
133 | 
134 | #### State Changes
135 | **File: [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts:27-39)**
136 | Add to `DeckState` interface:
137 | ```typescript
138 | loopEnabled: boolean;
139 | loopInNorm: number;  // normalized position 0..1
140 | loopOutNorm: number; // normalized position 0..1
141 | ```
142 | 
143 | **File: [`src/useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts:20-28)**
144 | Add new action types:
145 | ```typescript
146 | | { type: 'SET_LOOP_IN'; norm: number }
147 | | { type: 'SET_LOOP_OUT'; norm: number }
148 | | { type: 'TOGGLE_LOOP' }
149 | | { type: 'CLEAR_LOOP' }
150 | ```
151 | 
152 | #### Audio Graph Changes
153 | **File: [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts:118-149)**
154 | 
155 | The SPEC.md describes the loop implementation approach (lines 112-116):
156 | - Use **floored modulo** to wrap position into `[loopIn, loopOut)` range
157 | - Formula: `x - len·floor(x/len)` (not `el.mod` which uses `fmod`)
158 | - When loop is toggled off, re-base transport to continue from current position
159 | 
160 | Implementation in `buildDeckSignal`:
161 | ```typescript
162 | // After computing base position
163 | let position = el.add(base, el.accum(inc, seekTrig));
164 | 
165 | if (s.loopEnabled && s.loopInNorm < s.loopOutNorm) {
166 |   const loopLen = s.loopOutNorm - s.loopInNorm;
167 |   const loopStart = el.const({ key: `${s.id}_loopStart`, value: s.loopInNorm });
168 |   const loopLength = el.const({ key: `${s.id}_loopLen`, value: loopLen });
169 |   
170 |   // Offset position to loop start
171 |   const offset = el.sub(position, loopStart);
172 |   
173 |   // Floored modulo: x - len·floor(x/len)
174 |   const wrapped = el.sub(
175 |     offset,
176 |     el.mul(loopLength, el.floor(el.div(offset, loopLength)))
177 |   );
178 |   
179 |   // Add back loop start
180 |   position = el.add(loopStart, wrapped);
181 | }
182 | ```
183 | 
184 | #### UI Changes
185 | **File: [`src/components/DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)**
186 | Add loop controls:
187 | - **"Set In"** button - captures current position as loop-in
188 | - **"Set Out"** button - captures current position as loop-out
189 | - **"Loop"** toggle button - enables/disables looping
190 | - **"Clear"** button - removes loop points
191 | - Display current loop range (e.g., "Loop: 0:15 → 0:30")
192 | 
193 | ### Implementation Steps
194 | 1. Add loop state fields to [`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) `DeckState`
195 | 2. Update [`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) `initialDeckState` with default loop values
196 | 3. Add loop actions to [`useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts) reducer
197 | 4. Implement loop wrapping logic in [`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) `buildDeckSignal`
198 | 5. Handle loop exit: when toggling off, update `baseNorm` to current position
199 | 6. Add loop control buttons to [`DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)
200 | 7. Add loop callbacks to `UseDeck` interface
201 | 
202 | ### Testing Checklist
203 | - [ ] Set loop in/out points at different positions
204 | - [ ] Loop wraps seamlessly from out to in point
205 | - [ ] Loop toggle enables/disables correctly
206 | - [ ] Clear loop removes markers and disables looping
207 | - [ ] Loop works correctly with tempo changes
208 | - [ ] Exiting loop continues playback from current position
209 | - [ ] Loop points persist when pausing/playing
210 |

213 | ## Feature 3: In-Mix Cue Point
214 | 
215 | ### Description
216 | Allow users to set a cue point and jump back to it instantly. Essential for DJ workflow - mark the start of a drop, vocal, or beat.
217 | 
218 | ### Technical Approach
219 | 
220 | #### State Changes
221 | **File: [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts:27-39)**
222 | Add to `DeckState` interface:
223 | ```typescript
224 | cuePointNorm: number | null; // normalized position 0..1, null if not set
225 | ```
226 | 
227 | **File: [`src/useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts:20-28)**
228 | Add new action types:
229 | ```typescript
230 | | { type: 'SET_CUE' }        // Set cue at current position
231 | | { type: 'GO_TO_CUE' }      // Jump to cue point
232 | | { type: 'CLEAR_CUE' }      // Remove cue point
233 | ```
234 | 
235 | #### Transport Logic
236 | **File: [`src/useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts:32-54)**
237 | 
238 | Reducer implementation:
239 | ```typescript
240 | case 'SET_CUE':
241 |   return s.track ? { ...s, cuePointNorm: s.baseNorm } : s;
242 | 
243 | case 'GO_TO_CUE':
244 |   return s.cuePointNorm !== null
245 |     ? { ...s, baseNorm: s.cuePointNorm, seekGen: s.seekGen + 1 }
246 |     : s;
247 | 
248 | case 'CLEAR_CUE':
249 |   return { ...s, cuePointNorm: null };
250 | ```
251 | 
252 | Note: Use current `baseNorm` for setting cue (not `position` from state, which is async)
253 | 
254 | #### UI Changes
255 | **File: [`src/components/DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)**
256 | Add cue controls:
257 | - **"Set Cue"** button - marks current position
258 | - **"Cue"** button - jumps to cue point (highlighted when cue is set)
259 | - **"Clear Cue"** button - removes cue point
260 | - Visual indicator showing cue is set (e.g., button color change)
261 | 
262 | ### Implementation Steps
263 | 1. Add `cuePointNorm` to [`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) `DeckState`
264 | 2. Update [`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) `initialDeckState` with `cuePointNorm: null`
265 | 3. Add cue actions to [`useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts) reducer
266 | 4. Add cue control buttons to [`DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx)
267 | 5. Add cue callbacks to `UseDeck` interface
268 | 6. Ensure cue point is cleared when loading new track
269 | 
270 | ### Testing Checklist
271 | - [ ] Set cue point at various positions
272 | - [ ] Jump to cue point works instantly
273 | - [ ] Cue point persists during playback
274 | - [ ] Clear cue removes the marker
275 | - [ ] Cue point cleared when loading new track
276 | - [ ] Visual feedback shows when cue is set
277 | 
278 | ---
279 | 
280 | ## Feature 4: Visual Markers on Waveform
281 | 
282 | ### Description
283 | Display visual markers on the waveform for cue point and loop in/out points. This provides visual feedback for DJ workflow.
284 | 
285 | ### Technical Approach
286 | 
287 | #### Component Changes
288 | **File: [`src/components/Waveform.tsx`](src/components/Waveform.tsx)**
289 | 
290 | Current props:
291 | ```typescript
292 | interface Props {
293 |   peaks: TrackPeaks | null;
294 |   position: number;
295 |   onSeek: (norm: number) => void;
296 | }
297 | ```
298 | 
299 | Add new props:
300 | ```typescript
301 | interface Props {
302 |   peaks: TrackPeaks | null;
303 |   position: number;
304 |   onSeek: (norm: number) => void;
305 |   cuePoint: number | null;      // NEW
306 |   loopIn: number | null;         // NEW
307 |   loopOut: number | null;        // NEW
308 |   loopEnabled: boolean;          // NEW
309 | }
310 | ```
311 | 
312 | #### Rendering Logic
313 | **File: [`src/components/Waveform.tsx`](src/components/Waveform.tsx:82-126)**
314 | 
315 | Update the `draw` function to render markers after the waveform but before the playhead:
316 | 
317 | ```typescript
318 | // After blitting the waveform bitmap...
319 | 
320 | // Draw loop region (if enabled)
321 | if (loopEnabled && loopIn !== null && loopOut !== null) {
322 |   const loopInX = ((loopIn * total - start) / win) * cssW;
323 |   const loopOutX = ((loopOut * total - start) / win) * cssW;
324 |   
325 |   // Semi-transparent overlay
326 |   ctx.fillStyle = 'rgba(76, 194, 255, 0.2)';
327 |   ctx.fillRect(loopInX, 0, loopOutX - loopInX, cssH);
328 |   
329 |   // Loop in marker (green)
330 |   ctx.strokeStyle = '#4ade80';
331 |   ctx.lineWidth = 2;
332 |   ctx.beginPath();
333 |   ctx.moveTo(loopInX, 0);
334 |   ctx.lineTo(loopInX, cssH);
335 |   ctx.stroke();
336 |   
337 |   // Loop out marker (red)
338 |   ctx.strokeStyle = '#f87171';
339 |   ctx.beginPath();
340 |   ctx.moveTo(loopOutX, 0);
341 |   ctx.lineTo(loopOutX, cssH);
342 |   ctx.stroke();
343 | }
344 | 
345 | // Draw cue point marker (yellow)
346 | if (cuePoint !== null) {
347 |   const cueX = ((cuePoint * total - start) / win) * cssW;
348 |   ctx.strokeStyle = '#fbbf24';
349 |   ctx.lineWidth = 3;
350 |   ctx.beginPath();
351 |   ctx.moveTo(cueX, 0);
352 |   ctx.lineTo(cueX, cssH);
353 |   ctx.stroke();
354 |   
355 |   // Optional: Add triangle marker at top
356 |   ctx.fillStyle = '#fbbf24';
357 |   ctx.beginPath();
358 |   ctx.moveTo(cueX, 0);
359 |   ctx.lineTo(cueX - 5, 10);
360 |   ctx.lineTo(cueX + 5, 10);
361 |   ctx.closePath();
362 |   ctx.fill();
363 | }
364 | 
365 | // Draw playhead (existing code)...
366 | ```
367 | 
368 | #### Integration
369 | **File: [`src/components/DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx:63)**
370 | 
371 | Update Waveform component usage:
372 | ```typescript
373 | <Waveform
374 |   peaks={track?.peaks ?? null}
375 |   position={deck.position}
376 |   onSeek={deck.seek}
377 |   cuePoint={deck.state.cuePointNorm}
378 |   loopIn={deck.state.loopInNorm}
379 |   loopOut={deck.state.loopOutNorm}
380 |   loopEnabled={deck.state.loopEnabled}
381 | />
382 | ```
383 | 
384 | ### Visual Design
385 | - **Cue point**: Yellow vertical line with triangle marker (⚠️)
386 | - **Loop in**: Green vertical line (start of loop)
387 | - **Loop out**: Red vertical line (end of loop)
388 | - **Loop region**: Semi-transparent blue overlay between in/out points
389 | - **Playhead**: Red line (existing, should render on top)
390 | 
391 | ### Implementation Steps
392 | 1. Update [`Waveform.tsx`](src/components/Waveform.tsx) Props interface
393 | 2. Add marker rendering logic to `draw` function
394 | 3. Update [`DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx) to pass marker props
395 | 4. Test marker visibility at different zoom levels
396 | 5. Ensure markers scroll correctly with waveform
397 | 
398 | ### Testing Checklist
399 | - [ ] Cue point marker displays at correct position
400 | - [ ] Loop in/out markers display correctly
401 | - [ ] Loop region overlay shows between markers
402 | - [ ] Markers visible at all zoom levels
403 | - [ ] Markers scroll correctly with waveform
404 | - [ ] Markers clear when removed
405 | - [ ] Visual hierarchy: waveform → loop region → markers → playhead
406 | 
407 | ---
408 | 
409 | ## Integration & Dependencies
410 | 
411 | ### File Modification Summary
412 | 
413 | | File | Changes | Complexity |
414 | |------|---------|------------|
415 | | [`src/deck.ts`](src/deck.ts) | Add loop/cue state fields, implement loop wrapping | Medium |
416 | | [`src/useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts) | Add tempo/loop/cue actions and callbacks | Low |
417 | | [`src/components/DeckPanel.tsx`](src/components/DeckPanel.tsx) | Add tempo/loop/cue UI controls | Medium |
418 | | [`src/components/Waveform.tsx`](src/components/Waveform.tsx) | Add marker rendering | Medium |
419 | | [`src/index.css`](src/index.css) | Add styles for new controls | Low |
420 | 
421 | ### Dependency Graph
422 | 
423 | ```mermaid
424 | graph TD
425 |     A[Feature 1: Tempo Control] --> E[Testing]
426 |     B[Feature 2: Loop Functionality] --> D[Feature 4: Visual Markers]
427 |     C[Feature 3: Cue Point] --> D
428 |     D --> E
429 |     
430 |     style A fill:#a7f3d0
431 |     style B fill:#fde68a
432 |     style C fill:#fde68a
433 |     style D fill:#bfdbfe
434 |     style E fill:#e9d5ff
435 | ```
436 | 
437 | **Implementation Order:**
438 | 1. **Tempo Control** (independent, simplest)
439 | 2. **Cue Point** (independent, simple)
440 | 3. **Loop Functionality** (more complex, independent)
441 | 4. **Visual Markers** (depends on loop + cue state)
442 | 
443 | ---
444 | 
445 | ## Testing Strategy
446 | 
447 | ### Unit Testing Approach
448 | Each feature should be tested independently:
449 | 
450 | 1. **Tempo Control**
451 |    - Load track, adjust tempo slider
452 |    - Verify playback speed changes
453 |    - Check tempo resets on new track load
454 | 
455 | 2. **Loop Functionality**
456 |    - Set loop points, enable loop
457 |    - Verify seamless wraparound
458 |    - Test loop with different tempo settings
459 |    - Verify loop exit behavior
460 | 
461 | 3. **Cue Point**
462 |    - Set cue at various positions
463 |    - Jump to cue multiple times
464 |    - Verify cue persists during playback
465 | 
466 | 4. **Visual Markers**
467 |    - Set markers, verify visual display
468 |    - Test at different zoom levels
469 |    - Verify markers scroll with waveform
470 | 
471 | ### Integration Testing
472 | - Test all features together on both decks
473 | - Verify features work with crossfader
474 | - Test with different audio file formats
475 | - Verify no performance degradation
476 | 
477 | ### Edge Cases
478 | - Loop in > loop out (should be prevented or handled)
479 | - Cue point at track boundaries (0.0, 1.0)
480 | - Tempo changes during loop playback
481 | - Seeking while loop is enabled
482 | - Loading new track clears all markers
483 | 
484 | ---
485 | 
486 | ## Performance Considerations
487 | 
488 | ### Audio Graph Efficiency
489 | - Loop wrapping adds ~5 Elementary nodes per deck
490 | - Const nodes with stable keys prevent unnecessary graph rebuilds
491 | - No performance impact expected (graph is still small)
492 | 
493 | ### Canvas Rendering
494 | - Markers add minimal overhead (3-4 line draws per frame)
495 | - Existing cached bitmap approach remains efficient
496 | - No additional canvas allocations needed
497 | 
498 | ### State Updates
499 | - Tempo/loop/cue state changes trigger graph re-render
500 | - Elementary's diff algorithm keeps this efficient
501 | - High-frequency position updates remain in refs (not reducer)
502 | 
503 | ---
504 | 
505 | ## Known Limitations & Future Enhancements
506 | 
507 | ### Phase 4 Limitations
508 | - **Varispeed only**: Tempo and pitch change together (no time-stretching)
509 | - **No beat-phase sync**: SYNC feature comes in Phase 5
510 | - **Manual loop points**: No auto-loop or beat-quantized loops
511 | 
512 | ### Possible Phase 5+ Enhancements
513 | - BPM detection and beat-grid overlay
514 | - Tempo-match SYNC button
515 | - Beat-quantized loop points
516 | - Hot cues (multiple cue points)
517 | - Keyboard shortcuts for cue/loop
518 | 
519 | ---
520 | 
521 | ## Success Criteria
522 | 
523 | Phase 4 is complete when:
524 | - ✅ Tempo slider adjusts playback speed (0.5x–2.0x)
525 | - ✅ Loop in/out points can be set and enabled
526 | - ✅ Loop wraps seamlessly from out to in point
527 | - ✅ Cue point can be set and jumped to instantly
528 | - ✅ Visual markers display on waveform for cue/loop points
529 | - ✅ All features work on both decks independently
530 | - ✅ No audio artifacts or performance degradation
531 | - ✅ Features integrate cleanly with existing Phase 3 functionality
532 | 
533 | ---
534 | 
535 | ## Appendix: Code References
536 | 
537 | ### Key Elementary Audio Concepts Used
538 | 
539 | **Accumulator Transport** ([`deck.ts:128`](src/deck.ts:128))
540 | ```typescript
541 | position = base + accum(increment, seekGen)
542 | ```
543 | 
544 | **Floored Modulo for Loops** (SPEC.md:114)
545 | ```typescript
546 | wrapped = x - len·floor(x/len)
547 | ```
548 | 
549 | **Const Node Keys** ([`deck.ts:124-126`](src/deck.ts:124-126))
550 | ```typescript
551 | const inc = el.const({ key: `${s.id}_inc`, value: ... });
552 | ```
553 | Stable keys prevent graph rebuilds on value changes.
554 | 
555 | ### Relevant SPEC.md Sections
556 | - **§6: Deck Audio Model** (lines 91-120) - Transport architecture
557 | - **§8: Key Technical Decisions** (lines 142-167) - Graph ownership, state management
558 | - **§7: Phased Delivery** (lines 122-138) - Phase 4 description
559 | 
560 | ---
561 | 
562 | *This implementation plan is designed to be executed incrementally, with each feature testable independently before moving to the next.*
</content>

<file><path>ARCHITECTURE-QUIZ.md</path>
<content lines="1-258">
  1 | # 🔥 DeckFlow Web Architecture Quiz
  2 | 
  3 | A comprehensive set of questions to test deep understanding of the DeckFlow Web application's architecture, implementation decisions, and design tradeoffs.
  4 | 
  5 | ---
  6 | 
  7 | ## 🎯 Architecture & Design Questions
  8 | 
  9 | ### 1. Transport Model Deep Dive
 10 | **Question:** Your deck transport uses `position = base + accum(increment, seekGen)`. Why did you choose an audio-rate accumulator instead of controlling position from JavaScript? What would break if you tried to update position via React state at 60fps?
 11 | 
 12 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:6-14`](src/deck.ts:6-14)  
 13 | **Key concept:** Read the comment about "control-rate JS updates would zipper the audio"  
 14 | **Hint:** What happens when you change audio parameters at 60fps vs 48kHz sample rate?
 15 | 
 16 | ---
 17 | 
 18 | ### 2. Stereo Channel Strategy
 19 | **Question:** You store stereo tracks as two separate mono VFS entries (`A:L` and `A:R`) instead of one stereo buffer. This seems inefficient - why is this necessary given Elementary's `el.table` limitation? What would happen if you tried to use a single stereo VFS entry?
 20 | 
 21 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:16-17`](src/deck.ts:16-17) and [`track.ts:8-10`](src/track.ts:8-10)  
 22 | **Key limitation:** What does `el.table` read from a buffer?  
 23 | **Hint:** Check the comment about "el.table reads channel 0 only"
 24 | 
 25 | ---
 26 | 
 27 | ### 3. Loop Implementation
 28 | **Question:** Your loop uses a floored modulo (`x - len·floor(x/len)`) instead of `el.mod`. Why? What's wrong with `fmod` for this use case, and what audio artifacts would you hear if you used it instead?
 29 | 
 30 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:112-115`](SPEC.md:112-115)  
 31 | **Key concept:** "because `el.mod` is `fmod` and keeps the dividend's sign"  
 32 | **Hint:** What's the difference between `fmod(-0.1, 1.0)` and floored modulo?
 33 | 
 34 | ---
 35 | 
 36 | ### 4. Equal-Power Crossfade
 37 | **Question:** You use `gainA = cos(t·π/2)` and `gainB = sin(t·π/2)`. Why equal-power instead of linear crossfading? What would the user perceive differently at the center position with a linear fade?
 38 | 
 39 | **Find answer in:** [`App.tsx:47-50`](src/App.tsx:47-50) and [`SPEC.md:149-150`](SPEC.md:149-150)  
 40 | **Key concept:** What's "constant perceived loudness" and why does cos²+sin²=1 matter?  
 41 | **Hint:** At center position, both decks should be at ~0.707 gain
 42 | 
 43 | ---
 44 | 
 45 | ### 5. High-Frequency State Isolation
 46 | **Question:** Position and meter level live in refs + useState, never in the reducer. Why is this critical? What would happen to performance if you put `position` in the reducer that triggers the graph rebuild?
 47 | 
 48 | **Find answer in:** [`useDeck.ts:3-6`](src/useDeck.ts:3-6) and [`SPEC.md:151-153`](SPEC.md:151-153)  
 49 | **Key concept:** What happens if 30 updates/sec trigger graph re-renders?  
 50 | **Hint:** "High-frequency state stays out of the reducer"
 51 | 
 52 | ---
 53 | 
 54 | ### 6. Waveform Rendering Strategy
 55 | **Question:** You render peaks once to an offscreen canvas then blit slices. The comment mentions this fixed a "jank→message-backlog→crash spiral." Explain this failure mode - what was the chain of events that led to a crash?
 56 | 
 57 | **Find answer in:** [`Waveform.tsx:3-7`](src/components/Waveform.tsx:3-7) and [`SPEC.md:154-158`](SPEC.md:154-158)  
 58 | **Key concept:** Redrawing thousands of segments at 20Hz + audio events = ?  
 59 | **Hint:** "Cached waveform bitmap" and "50 ms renderer event-poll interval"
 60 | 
 61 | ---
 62 | 
 63 | ### 7. Varispeed vs Time-Stretch
 64 | **Question:** You chose varispeed (tempo and pitch linked) over real time-stretching. What DSP algorithm would you need to implement for independent pitch/tempo, and why is it "the hardest DSP" to add?
 65 | 
 66 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:33`](SPEC.md:33), [`SPEC.md:82-83`](SPEC.md:82-83), and [`SPEC.md:177-179`](SPEC.md:177-179)  
 67 | **Key terms:** WSOLA, signalsmith-stretch, WASM worklet  
 68 | **Hint:** "Avoids a WASM time-stretch (WSOLA / signalsmith) rabbit hole"
 69 | 
 70 | ---
 71 | 
 72 | ### 8. BPM Detection Approach
 73 | **Question:** Your BPM pipeline uses "energy-flux onset → autocorrelation → octave-fold into 80-160 BPM." Why autocorrelation specifically? What musical assumption does this make, and when would it fail catastrophically?
 74 | 
 75 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:162-166`](SPEC.md:162-166)  
 76 | **Key concept:** "peak lag = period"  
 77 | **Hint:** What musical structure does autocorrelation detect? What about non-periodic music?
 78 | 
 79 | ---
 80 | 
 81 | ### 9. Event Polling Rate
 82 | **Question:** You poll renderer events at 50ms intervals (20Hz). Why not faster for smoother playhead updates? What's the tradeoff you're managing here?
 83 | 
 84 | **Find answer in:** [`audio.ts:33-36`](src/audio.ts:33-36) and [`SPEC.md:154-158`](SPEC.md:154-158)  
 85 | **Key tradeoff:** Message rate vs main thread capacity  
 86 | **Hint:** "keeps the worklet→main message rate low enough that a busy main thread can't let the queue back up"
 87 | 
 88 | ---
 89 | 
 90 | ### 10. Seek Generation Counter
 91 | **Question:** `seekGen` is a monotonic counter that triggers transport resets when it changes by >0.5. Why use a counter instead of a boolean toggle? What edge case does this prevent?
 92 | 
 93 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:11-13`](src/deck.ts:11-13)  
 94 | **Key concept:** "changes by > 0.5"  
 95 | **Hint:** What if you seek twice in rapid succession with a boolean?
 96 | 
 97 | ---
 98 | 
 99 | ## 🎯 Implementation Challenges
100 | 
101 | ### 11. Race Condition
102 | **Question:** The `loadGen` counter guards against stale BPM results. Walk me through the exact race condition this prevents. What would the user experience if you removed this guard?
103 | 
104 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:166-167`](SPEC.md:166-167)  
105 | **Scenario:** Load track A → analysis starts → load track B → A's BPM arrives  
106 | **Hint:** "Worker race-guard"
107 | 
108 | ---
109 | 
110 | ### 12. Graph Diffing
111 | **Question:** You say "Elementary diffs against the previous graph, so unchanged nodes keep state." How does this work internally? What happens to a `el.lowpass` filter's internal state when you change an unrelated EQ value?
112 | 
113 | **Find answer in:** [`App.tsx:13-15`](src/App.tsx:13-15), [`deck.ts:21-22`](src/deck.ts:21-22), and [`deck.ts:123-124`](src/deck.ts:123-124)  
114 | **Key mechanism:** Stable `key` props on const nodes  
115 | **Hint:** "Const nodes carry stable keys so re-rendering only nudges their values"
116 | 
117 | ---
118 | 
119 | ### 13. AudioContext Resume
120 | **Question:** Why must `initAudio()` be called from a user gesture? What happens if you try to create an AudioContext on page load? Is this a security feature or a performance optimization?
121 | 
122 | **Find answer in:** [`audio.ts:18-21`](src/audio.ts:18-21)  
123 | **Browser policy:** Autoplay restrictions  
124 | **Hint:** "Must be called from a user gesture (click/tap) — browsers refuse to start an AudioContext otherwise"
125 | 
126 | ---
127 | 
128 | ### 14. Normalized Position
129 | **Question:** Everything uses normalized position (0..1) instead of frame counts. Why? What advantages does this give you, especially regarding sample rate conversions?
130 | 
131 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:16-17`](src/deck.ts:16-17) and [`track.ts:65-66`](src/track.ts:65-66)  
132 | **Key insight:** `el.table` maps [0,1] across the buffer  
133 | **Hint:** "decodeAudioData resamples to rt.ctx.sampleRate"
134 | 
135 | ---
136 | 
137 | ### 15. Loop Exit Behavior
138 | **Question:** When toggling loop off, the comment says "position will naturally continue from where it was" but you don't update `baseNorm`. How does the transport know where to continue from? Trace through the math.
139 | 
140 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:136-154`](src/deck.ts:136-154) and [`useDeck.ts:77-82`](src/useDeck.ts:77-82)  
141 | **Key insight:** Accumulator keeps integrating; position is already correct  
142 | **Hint:** The accumulator state persists even when the loop wrapping is removed from the graph
143 | 
144 | ---
145 | 
146 | ## 🚀 Extension & Scaling Questions
147 | 
148 | ### 16. Beat-Phase Sync
149 | **Question:** The spec mentions SYNC matches tempo but not beat alignment. How would you implement beat-phase sync? What additional data would you need to track, and where would you inject the phase correction?
150 | 
151 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:87-88`](SPEC.md:87-88) and [`SPEC.md:175-177`](SPEC.md:175-177)  
152 | **Hint:** BPM pipeline already computes "beat-phase from best-fitting pulse train"  
153 | **Key question:** How do you nudge the playhead to align downbeats?
154 | 
155 | ---
156 | 
157 | ### 17. AIFF Support
158 | **Question:** You mention AIFF works in Safari but fails in Chrome. If you had to implement a fallback decoder, what are the three hardest parts of parsing AIFF?
159 | 
160 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:172-175`](SPEC.md:172-175)  
161 | **Key challenges:** 
162 | 1. FORM/COMM/SSND parsing
163 | 2. Big-endian PCM
164 | 3. 80-bit extended sample rate
165 | 
166 | ---
167 | 
168 | ### 18. Multi-Output Routing
169 | **Question:** The browser gives you "one stereo bus." If you had access to a multi-channel audio interface, how would you route separate cue/headphone monitoring? What Web Audio API features would you use?
170 | 
171 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:38`](SPEC.md:38) and [`SPEC.md:76`](SPEC.md:76)  
172 | **Web Audio API:** `AudioContext.destination` channels, routing graph  
173 | **Hint:** "split monitoring needs multi-channel hardware"
174 | 
175 | ---
176 | 
177 | ### 19. Worklet Message Backpressure
178 | **Question:** What happens if the main thread gets blocked and can't consume meter/snapshot events fast enough? Does the AudioWorklet queue grow unbounded, or is there backpressure?
179 | 
180 | **Find answer in:** [`audio.ts:33-36`](src/audio.ts:33-36) and [`Waveform.tsx:3-7`](src/components/Waveform.tsx:3-7)  
181 | **Key insight:** Why 50ms interval was chosen  
182 | **Hint:** "a busy main thread can't let the queue back up"
183 | 
184 | ---
185 | 
186 | ### 20. Memory Management
187 | **Question:** When you load a new track, the old VFS entries (`A:L`, `A:R`) are replaced. Does Elementary automatically clean up the old buffers, or could you leak memory with repeated track loads?
188 | 
189 | **Find answer in:** [`track.ts:80-82`](src/track.ts:80-82)  
190 | **Key question:** Does `updateVirtualFileSystem` replace or append?  
191 | **Hint:** Look at how the VFS keys are structured with deck IDs
192 | 
193 | ---
194 | 
195 | ## 💡 Bonus Round: Show Me You Understand
196 | 
197 | ### 21. The Key Insight
198 | **Question:** The spec says the port was "unusually clean" because the mixer graph was already in portable JavaScript. Explain why this architectural decision in the desktop app made the browser port trivial. What would have been different if the EQ/filters were in C++?
199 | 
200 | **Find answer in:** [`SPEC.md:43-59`](SPEC.md:43-59) - "Why this port is tractable (the key insight)"  
201 | **Architecture:** Renderer-agnostic `el.*` code  
202 | **Key point:** "The entire mixer signal graph was already written in portable JavaScript"
203 | 
204 | ---
205 | 
206 | ### 22. Failure Mode: Numerical Precision
207 | **Question:** If a user loads a 2-hour DJ mix and sets tempo to 2.0x, what happens to the `increment` value? Could this cause numerical precision issues in the accumulator over time?
208 | 
209 | **Find answer in:** [`deck.ts:130`](src/deck.ts:130)  
210 | **Math challenge:** 
211 | - 2 hours = 7,200 seconds
212 | - At 48kHz = 345,600,000 samples
213 | - At 2.0x tempo = 691,200,000 accumulations
214 | - Float32 precision = ~7 significant digits
215 | 
216 | **Key question:** Does the accumulator drift? What's the actual precision loss?
217 | 
218 | ---
219 | 
220 | ## 📚 Study Guide
221 | 
222 | ### Essential Files to Master
223 | 1. **[`deck.ts`](src/deck.ts)** - Core audio graph and transport model
224 | 2. **[`useDeck.ts`](src/useDeck.ts)** - State management and event handling
225 | 3. **[`audio.ts`](src/audio.ts)** - Web Audio + Elementary initialization
226 | 4. **[`track.ts`](src/track.ts)** - File loading and VFS management
227 | 5. **[`App.tsx`](src/App.tsx)** - Graph composition and crossfading
228 | 6. **[`Waveform.tsx`](src/components/Waveform.tsx)** - Performance-critical rendering
229 | 7. **[`SPEC.md`](SPEC.md)** - Complete architectural documentation
230 | 
231 | ### Key Concepts to Understand
232 | - **Audio-rate vs control-rate** signal processing
233 | - **Elementary's graph diffing** and node keying
234 | - **VFS (Virtual File System)** in Elementary
235 | - **Equal-power crossfading** mathematics
236 | - **Accumulator-based transport** model
237 | - **Event polling** and backpressure management
238 | - **Canvas rendering optimization** techniques
239 | - **React state management** for high-frequency updates
240 | 
241 | ### Advanced Topics
242 | - **DSP algorithms:** WSOLA, time-stretching, autocorrelation
243 | - **Web Audio API:** AudioContext, AudioWorklet, WASM
244 | - **Browser constraints:** Autoplay policies, single output bus
245 | - **Performance optimization:** Offscreen canvas, event throttling
246 | - **Race conditions:** Async loading, worker communication
247 | 
248 | ---
249 | 
250 | ## 🎓 How to Use This Quiz
251 | 
252 | 1. **Self-Study:** Try answering each question before looking at the hints
253 | 2. **Code Review:** Use the file references to verify your understanding
254 | 3. **Deep Dive:** Follow the hint chains to understand the full context
255 | 4. **Discussion:** Use these questions to explain the architecture to others
256 | 5. **Interview Prep:** These cover real architectural decisions and tradeoffs
257 | 
258 | **Remember:** The goal isn't just to know *what* the code does, but *why* it's designed this way and what would break if you changed it.
</content>

<content># 🎧 DeckFlow Web - DJ Mixer Application

A browser-based two-deck DJ mixer built with React, TypeScript, and Elementary Audio. This project demonstrates professional audio graph architecture, real-time DSP, and modern web audio techniques.

## 🎯 Project Overview

DeckFlow Web is a teaching-focused implementation of a dual-deck DJ mixer that runs entirely in the browser. It showcases:

- **Dual-deck audio playback** with independent transport controls
- **Real-time audio processing** using Elementary Audio's declarative DSP
- **Professional mixer features**: 3-band EQ, DJ filter, crossfader, volume control
- **Visual feedback**: Waveform display with playhead tracking and zoom
- **DJ workflow features**: Varispeed tempo control, manual loops, cue points
- **Performance optimization**: Cached canvas rendering, audio-rate transport

## ✨ Features Implemented

### Phase 3: Two-Deck Mixer (Completed)
- ✅ Dual independent decks with play/pause/seek
- ✅ Per-deck signal chain: 3-band EQ + DJ LPF/HPF filter + volume + level meter
- ✅ Equal-power crossfader for smooth transitions
- ✅ Master volume control
- ✅ Canvas waveform visualization with zoom
- ✅ Click-to-seek functionality

### Phase 4: DJ Features (Completed)
- ✅ **Varispeed tempo control** (0.5x - 2.0x range)
- ✅ **Manual loop functionality** with seamless wraparound
- ✅ **In-mix cue points** for instant position recall
- ✅ **Visual markers** on waveform (cue point, loop in/out)

## 🏗️ Architecture Highlights

### Audio Graph Design
- **Accumulator-based transport**: Audio-rate position tracking prevents zipper noise
- **Stereo handling**: Dual mono VFS entries for Elementary's `el.table` limitation
- **Loop implementation**: Floored modulo wrapping for seamless loop playback
- **Equal-power crossfade**: Constant perceived loudness using cos/sin curves

### Performance Optimizations
- **Cached waveform rendering**: Offscreen canvas bitmap prevents redraw overhead
- **High-frequency state isolation**: Position/meter updates in refs, not reducer
- **Event polling**: 50ms interval prevents message queue backpressure
- **Graph diffing**: Elementary's stable keys minimize unnecessary rebuilds

### Key Technical Decisions
- **Varispeed only**: Tempo and pitch linked (no time-stretching) for simplicity
- **Browser-native decode**: `AudioContext.decodeAudioData()` for WAV/MP3/FLAC
- **Single stereo output**: No split cue/headphone monitoring
- **In-memory session**: No persistence layer

## 🚀 Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- Node.js 16+ and npm

### Installation & Running
```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start development server
npm run dev

# Build for production
npm run build
```

Open the provided URL and click **"Load track"** to begin (audio requires user gesture).

### Supported Audio Formats
- WAV (uncompressed PCM)
- MP3
- FLAC
- AIFF (Safari only)

## 📁 Project Structure

```
src/
├── audio.ts              # AudioContext + WebRenderer initialization
├── track.ts              # File decode → VFS + peaks generation
├── deck.ts               # DeckState + buildDeckSignal (transport, EQ, loop)
├── useDeck.ts            # Per-deck hook: reducer, transport, events
├── App.tsx               # Two-deck composition + crossfader + master
└── components/
    ├── Waveform.tsx      # Cached canvas: peaks + markers + playhead
    ├── DeckPanel.tsx     # Deck UI: load, waveform, transport, cue/loop
    ├── DeckControls.tsx  # Mixer strip: EQ + filter + tempo + volume
    ├── Knob.tsx          # Rotary control component
    ├── Fader.tsx         # Linear slider component
    └── Mixer.tsx         # Crossfader + master volume
```

## 🎓 Learning Resources

This project includes comprehensive documentation:

- **[`SPEC.md`](SPEC.md)** - Complete architectural specification and design rationale
- **[`implementation-plan.md`](implementation-plan.md)** - Phase 4 implementation guide
- **[`ARCHITECTURE-QUIZ.md`](ARCHITECTURE-QUIZ.md)** - 22 deep-dive questions testing understanding
- **[`conversation-log.json`](conversation-log.json)** - Development session transcript

## 🛠️ Tech Stack

- **Frontend**: React 18 + TypeScript (strict mode)
- **Build**: Vite
- **Audio**: Elementary Audio (`@elemaudio/core` + `@elemaudio/web-renderer`)
- **Canvas**: Native HTML5 Canvas API
- **State**: React hooks + useReducer pattern

## 🎯 Design Philosophy

This project prioritizes **clarity and teachability** over production fidelity:

- **Readable end-to-end**: Clone, install, understand in an afternoon
- **No black boxes**: All DSP logic visible in Elementary nodes
- **Incremental complexity**: Each phase builds on the previous
- **Real-world patterns**: Professional audio architecture scaled down

## 🔧 Known Limitations

- **Varispeed only**: No independent tempo/pitch control (would require WASM time-stretch)
- **No beat-phase sync**: SYNC matches tempo only, not beat alignment
- **Single output**: No split cue/headphone monitoring
- **No persistence**: Session data lost on reload
- **Manual BPM**: No automatic beat detection (planned for Phase 5)

## 🚀 Future Enhancements (Phase 5+)

- BPM detection in Web Worker
- Beat-grid overlay on waveform
- Tempo-match SYNC button
- Tap-tempo functionality
- Session library with drag-to-deck
- Beat-quantized loop points

## 👥 Development Team

Built as part of the IBM July Challenge hands-on lab series, demonstrating:
- Modern web audio architecture
- React state management patterns
- Performance optimization techniques
- Professional DSP implementation

## 📄 License

This project is part of the IBM SkillsBuild AI Builders Challenge educational materials.

## 🙏 Acknowledgments

- **Elementary Audio** for the declarative DSP framework
- **IBM SkillsBuild** for the challenge structure
- Original **DeckFlow** desktop app architecture (Electron + C++ + Elementary)

---

**Ready to mix?** Load two tracks, adjust the EQ, set some loops, and blend them with the crossfader. This is a fully functional DJ mixer running entirely in your browser! 🎵
</content>

<content># 🚀 IBM July Challenge - Hands-On Labs

This repository contains hands-on lab projects completed as part of the IBM SkillsBuild AI Builders Challenge (July 2026).

## 📚 Projects

### 03_djapp_july - DeckFlow Web DJ Mixer

A professional browser-based two-deck DJ mixer application demonstrating advanced web audio architecture and real-time DSP.

**[View Project →](03_djapp_july/)**

#### Key Features
- 🎵 Dual-deck audio playback with independent controls
- 🎛️ Professional mixer: 3-band EQ, DJ filter, crossfader, volume control
- 📊 Real-time waveform visualization with zoom
- 🔄 Varispeed tempo control (0.5x - 2.0x)
- 🔁 Manual loop functionality with visual markers
- 📍 In-mix cue points for instant position recall

#### Tech Stack
- React 18 + TypeScript
- Elementary Audio (declarative DSP)
- Vite build system
- HTML5 Canvas API

#### Architecture Highlights
- Audio-rate accumulator transport for glitch-free playback
- Equal-power crossfading for constant perceived loudness
- Cached canvas rendering for optimal performance
- High-frequency state isolation pattern

#### Learning Resources
- **[SPEC.md](03_djapp_july/SPEC.md)** - Complete architectural specification
- **[implementation-plan.md](03_djapp_july/implementation-plan.md)** - Phase 4 implementation guide
- **[ARCHITECTURE-QUIZ.md](03_djapp_july/ARCHITECTURE-QUIZ.md)** - 22 deep-dive architecture questions
- **[conversation-log.json](03_djapp_july/conversation-log.json)** - Development session transcript

## 🎯 Challenge Goals

This hands-on lab series demonstrates:
- Modern web application architecture
- Real-time audio processing in the browser
- Performance optimization techniques
- Professional state management patterns
- Comprehensive technical documentation

## 🛠️ Getting Started

Each project includes its own README with specific setup instructions. Generally:

```bash
cd 03_djapp_july
npm install
npm run dev
```

## 👥 Team

Built by the IBM July Challenge team, showcasing:
- Advanced React patterns
- Web Audio API expertise
- DSP implementation
- Performance optimization
- Technical documentation

## 📄 Documentation Philosophy

Each project includes:
- **Specification documents** explaining architectural decisions
- **Implementation plans** with step-by-step guides
- **Architecture quizzes** testing deep understanding
- **Conversation logs** showing the development process

## 🙏 Acknowledgments

- **IBM SkillsBuild** for the challenge framework
- **Elementary Audio** for the declarative DSP library
- **Open source community** for the tools and libraries

---

**Explore the projects** to see professional web development patterns in action! 🎵
